title: How to Deal with Spiritual Comparison
meta_description: Discover why spiritual comparison is toxic and how to stop measuring your path against others. This article helps you reclaim your unique journey without the pressure.
og_title: How to Deal with Spiritual Comparison
og_description: Discover why spiritual comparison is toxic and how to stop measuring your path against others. This article helps you reclaim your unique journey without the pressure.
body:
You're Not Behind. You're Just Not Them.

Let's be real for a second. You're scrolling through your feed, and there she is again. The girl who channels angels in her sleep. The guy who can read your past lives like a menu. The woman who's been meditating for three hours a day since she was six, and now she's teaching workshops in Bali while you're trying to figure out if that weird tingle in your third eye is just a sinus headache.

You close the app. You feel a little sick. You tell yourself you're happy for them. But underneath, there's this gnawing feeling. A voice that whispers, "You're not doing it right. You're not spiritual enough. You're broken."

I know that voice. I've had it scream at me for years. And here's the truth nobody wants to say out loud: spiritual comparison is one of the most toxic games you can play, and it will absolutely wreck your development if you let it.

You're not here to be anyone else's version of enlightened. You're here to be your own. And that's a completely different thing. So let's talk about how to deal with this, not with fluffy platitudes, but with real, grounded strategies that actually work.

Where Does This Comparison Come From? (And Why It's Not Your Fault)

First off, let's name the beast. Spiritual comparison isn't just regular jealousy dressed up in hemp and crystals. It's something deeper. It comes from a place that's actually very human, even though we're talking about spiritual stuff.

Think about it. You grew up in a world that taught you to measure yourself against others. Your grades, your looks, your job, your relationship status. Everything was a competition, whether you wanted it to be or not. Then you wake up one day and realize there's a whole other layer to life - the spiritual layer. And guess what? Your brain, bless its little pattern-matching heart, immediately tries to turn that into another competition too.

"Oh, you're more intuitive than me? Well, I'll just meditate harder." "They can see auras? I can barely see my own hand in the dark."

Does that land? Because it should. This is not a sign that you're a bad person. It's a sign that your nervous system is trying to keep you safe by using the only approach it knows - comparison. Your brain thinks that if you can figure out where you rank on the spiritual ladder, you'll know if you're safe. But there is no ladder. There never was.

The real issue is that spiritual development, by its very nature, is personal. It's not a race. It's not a competition. And yet, because we're social creatures, we can't help but look sideways and wonder how we're doing compared to everyone else.

The Myth of the "Advanced" Spiritual Person

Let me tell you something that might piss you off. The people who look like they have it all together spiritually? They don't. I don't care if they're channeling, healing, or levitating (okay, probably not levitating). They have their own mess. They have their own blind spots. They have their own days where they feel like a total fraud.

I remember sitting in a workshop once, watching a woman who had been doing this work for thirty years. She was calm, centered, and seemed to radiate this light that made everyone around her feel safe. I felt like a toddler next to her. A clumsy, fumbling toddler who couldn't even sit still for five minutes.

After the session, I walked up to her, expecting some deep wisdom. Instead, she looked at me with tired eyes and said, "I still fight with my husband. I still eat too much sugar when I'm stressed. And last week, I had a panic attack in the grocery store because I couldn't decide which brand of almond milk to buy."

That was the moment I realized: nobody is as advanced as they look from the outside. We all have our stuff. The only difference is that some people are better at hiding it, or they've learned to talk about it in a way that sounds spiritual instead of messy.

"The person you're comparing yourself to is also comparing themselves to someone else. The whole thing is a hall of mirrors. The only way out is to stop looking at the mirrors and start looking at yourself."

Your Path Is Yours. Period.

Here's a hard truth that you need to sit with: your spiritual path is not supposed to look like anyone else's. Not even close. And the moment you try to make it match, you break the whole thing.

Think about it this way. You have a unique combination of experiences, wounds, strengths, and soul contracts (if you believe in that sort of thing). You have a specific nervous system that's calibrated a certain way. You have a particular set of traumas that shaped you, and a particular set of gifts that came from those traumas. Nobody else on this planet has that exact combination.

So why would you expect your spiritual journey to look like theirs? It doesn't even make logical sense. It's like comparing a maple tree to an oak tree and saying one is "more treelike." They're both trees. They're both growing. But they're doing it in completely different ways, at completely different rates, and producing completely different results.

You might be a slow, deep rooter. Someone who takes years to integrate a single insight, but when you do, it's solid as stone. Or you might be a fast, branching expander. Someone who picks up new practices every month and weaves them together into something wild and beautiful. Neither is better. They're just different.

When you compare, you're trying to force your tree to grow like theirs. And all that does is stunt your growth.

Practical Steps to Stop the Comparison Loop

Okay, so we've named the problem. Now let's get into the actual work. Because knowing that comparison is bad doesn't make it stop. You need tools. Real tools that you can use when that familiar knot forms in your stomach.

1. Get Honest About What You're Really Feeling

The next time you feel that spike of comparison, stop. Don't scroll away. Don't judge yourself. Just sit with the feeling for sixty seconds and ask yourself: "What am I actually feeling?"

Is it jealousy? Is it fear? Is it loneliness? Is it a sense of inadequacy that's been there since you were a kid? Because nine times out of ten, the spiritual comparison is just a mask for something older. Something that was there long before you ever picked up a crystal or started meditating.

For me, it was always about feeling like I wasn't enough. That voice started in childhood, long before I knew what intuition was. The spiritual stuff just gave it a new playground. When I realized that, it lost a lot of its power. I wasn't jealous of the angel channeler. I was just running an old tape.

So get honest. Name the real feeling. And then tell yourself: "This is an old wound. It's not about them. It's about me."

2. Stop Following People Who Make You Feel Small

This one is simple but brutal. If someone's content consistently makes you feel like crap about your own path, unfollow them. Mute them. Block them if you have to. I don't care if they're the most "enlightened" person on the planet. If their energy makes you feel like you're behind, they are not serving you right now.

You can come back to them later when you're more solid in your own foundation. Or you can never come back. That's fine too. There is no spiritual obligation to consume content that hurts you.

select your feed like you're curating a garden. Pull out the weeds. Plant what nourishes you. And if you're worried about missing out on something important, trust me - the real teachers don't need to make you feel small to teach you. The real ones make you feel seen.

3. Create Your Own Practice, Not Theirs

Here's where it gets practical. Instead of trying to copy someone else's routine, start building one that actually fits you. And I mean fits you, not the version of you that you think you should be.

Do you hate meditating for long periods? Don't do it. Try walking meditation, or dancing, or just sitting outside for five minutes. Does journaling feel like a chore? Stop. Try voice memos, or drawing, or just thinking before you fall asleep.

Your practice should feel like coming home, not like homework. And if it doesn't, you're doing someone else's practice.

One of the best books I've found for this is The Awakened Brain by Lisa Miller. It talks about how spirituality is actually wired into our biology, and that everyone's expression of it is different. It's not about following a script. It's about finding your own signal in the noise. See on Amazon (paid link)

4. Measure Progress by Your Own Standards

If you're going to measure your spiritual growth - and let's be honest, most of us do it anyway - at least use the right measuring stick. Don't measure by how many followers you have, how many chakras you can feel, or how many past lives you can remember. Measure by things that actually matter.

Ask yourself: "Am I more kind than I was a year ago? Am I more patient? Do I judge myself less? Do I judge others less? Do I feel more connected to life, even when it's hard?"

Those are the real metrics. And they're invisible to the outside world. Nobody can see your inner shift. But you can feel it. And that's the only thing that counts.

5. Get Grounded in Your Body

Spiritual comparison lives in the head. It's all thoughts, stories, and mental images of who you "should" be. The fastest way out of it is to drop into your body. Right now. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel your breath moving in and out. Feel the weight of your hands in your lap.

When you're in your body, you can't compare. Because comparison requires a story, and the body doesn't do stories. It just does sensations. So when the comparison monster rears its head, get physical. Stomp your feet. Shake your hands. Breathe deep. Come back to the present moment where you're just a person, alive, doing your best.

This is where a book like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk can be a breakthrough. It explains why trauma and comparison live in the body, and how to actually release them instead of just thinking your way out. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Gift of Your Own Timing

Here's something I wish someone had told me years ago: your timing is not a mistake. You're not supposed to be further along than you are. You're not supposed to have it all figured out. You're exactly where you need to be, even if it doesn't feel like it.

Think about a seed. When you plant it, you don't dig it up every day to check if it's growing. You trust the process. You know that underground, in the dark, things are happening. Roots are spreading. The seed is breaking open. It looks like nothing from the surface, but everything is happening.

That's you. Right now. You might feel like you're in the dark, like nothing is happening, like everyone else is blooming while you're still just dirt. But things are happening. You're changing. You're expanding. You just can't see it yet because it's happening underground.

And when you do bloom, it will be in your own time, in your own way, and it will be exactly right for you. Not a moment too soon. Not a moment too late.

What If Comparison Is Actually a Teacher?

Okay, I want to offer you one more perspective. This one is a little more advanced, but I think you're ready for it. What if the people you compare yourself to are actually your teachers? Not in the way that you should copy them, but in the way that they show you what you're ready to call in.

When you feel that spike of jealousy, ask yourself: "What do they have that I want to experience?" Maybe it's confidence. Maybe it's clarity. Maybe it's a sense of purpose. Whatever it is, that's not about them. That's about you. That's a signal from your own soul, pointing you toward what's next for you.

So instead of shaming yourself for the comparison, thank it. Say, "Oh, I see. I'm ready to grow in that direction. Thank you for showing me." And then get to work on your own version of it. Not their version. Yours.

This reframe changes everything. Suddenly, the person you were jealous of becomes a mirror instead of a threat. And mirrors don't compete with you. They just show you what's already there.

Building a Relationship With Your Own Intuition

when it comes down to it, spiritual comparison is a symptom of one thing: you haven't fully trusted your own inner guidance yet. You're still looking outside for validation, for signs that you're on the right track. And that's normal. That's human. But it's also something you can heal.

The more you practice listening to your own intuition - even when it's quiet, even when it's messy, even when it doesn't make sense - the less you'll need to compare. Because you'll know, deep down, that you have your own compass. And that compass is pointing you exactly where you need to go.

One book that really helped me with this is Spiritual Liberation by Michael Bernard Beckwith. It's not about comparing yourself to anyone. It's about realizing that your spiritual journey is about freedom, not achievement. And that freedom comes from within, not from being better than someone else. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Final Word (For When It Gets Hard)

I'm not going to pretend that this is easy. Spiritual comparison is one of the most persistent patterns I've seen in myself and in the people I work with. It comes back. It sneaks in when you're feeling vulnerable. It whispers in your ear when you're tired or lonely or scared.

But here's the thing. Every time you notice it, every time you catch yourself and bring yourself back to your own path, you're doing the work. You're strengthening your own foundation. You're building trust in yourself. And that trust is the only thing that will ever truly silence the comparison voice.

So keep going. Keep coming back to yourself. Keep choosing your own path, even when it feels small or slow or invisible. Because it's yours. And that makes it the most important path in the world.

You're not behind. You're not broken. You're just on your own journey, at your own pace, in your own way. And that is exactly how it's supposed to be.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've put together a short assessment that can help you identify where you're holding onto comparison patterns and how to start releasing them. You can find it at IntuitivesAwaken.com/assessment. It's free, it's quick, and it might give you the clarity you've been looking for.
